title: Inhaled fluticasone reduces sputum inflammatory indices in severe bronchiectasis.
abstract: Although corticosteroid therapy might be clinically beneficial for bronchiectasis, very little is known of its effects on the inflammatory and infective markers in bronchiectasis. We have therefore performed a double-blind, placebo-controlled study to evaluate the effects of a 4-wk administration of inhaled fluticasone in bronchiectasis. Twenty-four patients (12 female; mean age 51 yr) were randomized into receiving either inhaled fluticasone (500 microgram twice daily) via the Accuhaler device (n = 12) or placebo. At each visit, spirometry, 24-h sputum volume, sputum leukocyte density, bacterial densities, and concentrations of interleukin (IL)-1beta, IL-8, tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-alpha), and leukotriene B4 (LTB4) were determined. There was a significant (p 0.05) or any reported adverse reactions in either group. The results of this study show that high-dose fluticasone is effective in reducing the sputum inflammatory indices in bronchiectasis. Large-scale and long-term studies are indicated to evaluate the effects of inhaled steroid therapy on the inflammatory components in bronchiectasis.
journal: American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine

screening.reason: The study evaluates the effects of inhaled fluticasone on bronchiectasis, which is relevant to the review objective.

overall_concept_summary: This double-blind, placebo-controlled trial investigates the impact of high-dose inhaled fluticasone on sputum inflammatory indices in severe bronchiectasis patients.
detailed_summary: The study found that inhaled fluticasone significantly reduced sputum inflammatory markers in severe bronchiectasis patients over a four-week period, suggesting potential benefits for managing the condition through corticosteroid therapy.
methods_summary: A double-blind, placebo-controlled trial with 24 participants (12 receiving fluticasone and 12 receiving placebo) was conducted to assess the effects of inhaled fluticasone on sputum inflammatory indices over a four-week period.
results_summary: Fluticasone treatment led to significant reductions in sputum leukocyte density, bacterial densities, and concentrations of IL-1beta, IL-8, TNF-alpha, and LTB4 compared to the placebo group.
conclusion_summary: High-dose fluticasone is effective in reducing sputum inflammatory indices in severe bronchiectasis patients, indicating potential benefits for managing the condition through corticosteroid therapy.
key_takeaways: Inhaled fluticasone reduces sputum inflammatory markers in severe bronchiectasis patients.
Further large-scale and long-term studies are needed to evaluate the effects of inhaled steroid therapy on bronchiectasis.
population_or_sample: 24 patients with severe bronchiectasis (12 female; mean age 51 yr)
intervention_or_exposure: Inhaled fluticasone (500 microgram twice daily) via the Accuhaler device
outcomes_measured: Concentrations of IL-1beta, IL-8, TNF-alpha, and LTB4
main_results: Significant reductions in sputum inflammatory markers with fluticasone treatment compared to placebo.
conclusions: High-dose fluticasone is effective in reducing sputum inflammatory indices in severe bronchiectasis patients.
Further large-scale and long-term studies are indicated.
evidence_quotes_or_locations: The results of this study show that high-dose fluticasone is effective in reducing the sputum inflammatory indices in bronchiectasis.
